coredata.dc:title: Sustainable synthesis of Cu-doped carbon dots from biomass waste for sensitive molnupiravir analysis in pharmaceutical capsules
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Elshenawy, Eman A.
Mansour, Fotouh R.
coredata.dc:description: Exploring safe alternatives to substitute environmentally detrimental chemical reagents in analytical procedures is as important as developing affordable analytical tools. This work presents a straightforward method using carbon quantum dots (CDs) as fluorescence probes for the sensitive and eco-friendly detection of molnupiravir, an anti-influenza drug showing promise against COVID-19. The research introduces a novel, rapid, and selective fluorescent probe for molnupiravir determination, derived from biowaste carbon dots (CDs). These CDs were synthesized via a green, simple, one-step microwave pyrolysis process in just 3 min, utilizing copper-doped roasted pulp seed peels to create the Cu-CDs sensor. This energy-efficient method produced CDs with a narrow particle size distribution. The environmental friendliness of the method was evaluated using MoGAPI and AGREE metrics, proving it to be exceptionally green. The Cu-CDs were applied as nanoprobes for detecting molnupiravir in its commercial formulation, utilizing a static quenching mechanism. The probe demonstrated excellent performance for molnupiravir detection in marketed capsules, with a linearity range of 9–120 mM (r = 0.9985). Validation studies confirmed the method’s accuracy (98.33–101.53 % recovery), precision (≤1.92 % RSD), robustness, and reliability. Different evaluation metrics were used to compare the eco-friendliness and applicability of this method with existing approaches. Additionally, CDs were effectively used for the accurate analysis of molnupiravir in capsule dosage forms, with results demonstrating that the method is more environmentally friendly and more applicable than other techniques, achieving an average recovery of 101.47 ± 1.95 %.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molnupiravir
Microwave-assisted synthesis
Biowaste-derived carbon dots
Copper-doped carbon dots
Green preparation
Static quenching

Studying the sensing mechanism of molnupiravir
Green assessment of the proposed method

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Procedures of molnupiravir determination in pharmaceutical capsules using Cu-CDs.
TEM image (A), HRTEM image (figure inset shows particle size distribution) (B), EDX (C) and FTIR (D) of the prepared Cu-CDs.
Effect of pH on the fluorescence ratio at xxx nm of Cu-CDs in absence (F0) and presence (F) of molnupiravir.
Effect of excipients on the emission of Cu-CDs.
Greenness and practicality assessments of the Cu-CDs method using AGREEprep, AGREE and BAGI.
Validation results of the presented Cu-CDs for molnupiravir detection.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Sa: intercept’s standard deviation; Sb, slope’s standard deviation and Sy/x: standard deviation of residuals.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of comparison between the developed Cu-CDs method, and the reference method.

head.ce:title: Sustainable synthesis of Cu-doped carbon dots from biomass waste for sensitive molnupiravir analysis in pharmaceutical capsules

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy-Tanta University, Tanta 31111, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy-Tanta University, Tanta 31111, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Medicinal Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, King Salman International University, Ras Sudr 46612, South Sinai, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Medicinal Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, King Salman International University, Ras Sudr, South Sinai 8745101, Egypt

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy-Tanta University, Tanta 31111, Egypt.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Exploring safe alternatives to substitute environmentally detrimental chemical reagents in analytical procedures is as important as developing affordable analytical tools. This work presents a straightforward method using carbon quantum dots (CDs) as fluorescence probes for the sensitive and eco-friendly detection of molnupiravir, an anti-influenza drug showing promise against COVID-19. The research introduces a novel, rapid, and selective fluorescent probe for molnupiravir determination, derived from biowaste carbon dots (CDs). These CDs were synthesized via a green, simple, one-step microwave pyrolysis process in just 3 min, utilizing copper-doped roasted pulp seed peels to create the Cu-CDs sensor. This energy-efficient method produced CDs with a narrow particle size distribution. The environmental friendliness of the method was evaluated using MoGAPI and AGREE metrics, proving it to be exceptionally green. The Cu-CDs were applied as nanoprobes for detecting molnupiravir in its commercial formulation, utilizing a static quenching mechanism. The probe demonstrated excellent performance for molnupiravir detection in marketed capsules, with a linearity range of 9–120 mM (r = 0.9985). Validation studies confirmed the method’s accuracy (98.33–101.53 % recovery), precision (≤1.92 % RSD), robustness, and reliability. Different evaluation metrics were used to compare the eco-friendliness and applicability of this method with existing approaches. Additionally, CDs were effectively used for the accurate analysis of molnupiravir in capsule dosage forms, with results demonstrating that the method is more environmentally friendly and more applicable than other techniques, achieving an average recovery of 101.47 ± 1.95 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the era of green analytical chemistry, there is a strong interest in developing new methods for pharmaceutical analysis that are sustainable and ecologically friendly. This has led to a focus on using waste-derived materials in innovative ways. The COVID-19 pandemic has highlighted the urgent requirement for fast and accurate analytical methods, especially for antiviral drugs such as molnupiravir, which has shown promise as a treatment for SARS-CoV-2 [1–5]. Conventional techniques for drug analysis, such as LC-MS/MS [6,7], HPLC [8], are effective but have drawbacks in terms of environmental and health hazards because they use organic solvents and complicated instruments. Capillary electrophoresis [9] was also applied for molnupiravir determination, but the instrument is not available in most quality control laboratories. Hence, it is imperative to investigate environmentally friendly and more effective alternatives. Spectrofluorometry in this context provides a significant benefit due to its simplicity, sensitivity, cost efficiency, and minimum impact on the environment [10].

Carbon dots (CDs) are novel materials formed of sp2 hybridized carbon core and polar surfaces formed of various functional groups ranging from hydroxyl to carbonyl and amine groups [11]. Traditionally, CDs are prepared from organic precursors such as citric acid [12], glucose, and uric acid [13]. Doping with heteroatoms was found to boast the quantum yield and enhance the photoluminescent properties of CDs [14,15]. Precursors such as cysteine [16], methionine [17], and thiosemicarbazide [18,19] have been frequently used as nitrogen and sulfur sources. The current trends to replace chemicals with more sustainable materials encouraged scientist to prepare CDs for biomass waste [20–22]. These biowaste-based CDs proved to be innovative and environmentally friendly method for producing fluorescent nanoprobes [23–26]. These CD sensors are environmentally benign and have distinctive physicochemical and optical characteristics, making them excellent options for a wide range of analytical applications. Doping with metals substantially altered the electronic structure and the energy levels of CDs which was reflected in the improved the luminescent characteristics and the tuned selectivity of these nanoprobes [27,28].

This study presents a novel spectrofluorometric technique for quantifying molnupiravir in pharmaceutical capsules. The method utilizes carbon dots derived from discarded pulp-seeds peels, demonstrating a new approach. Using a green, one-step microwave pyrolysis method, the waste peels are converted into copper-doped carbon dots (Cu-CDs) that emit a strong light within a short period of time. The Cu-CDs have remarkable fluorescence characteristics and are employed as innovative fluorescent nanoprobes for the precise and specific identification of molnupiravir.

The developed approach is distinguished by its speed, simplicity, and eco-friendliness, effectively meeting the urgent demand for more environmentally sustainable analytical procedures in pharmaceutical analysis. Moreover, the utilization of carbon dots obtained from trash not only encourages the valorization of waste but also adheres to the principles of green chemistry. The results of the study highlight the capability of Cu-CDs as efficient nanoprobes, providing a strong and environmentally friendly option for accurately measuring molnupiravir in pharmaceutical formulations. This innovative method not only improves the ability to analyze antiviral medications but also supports the wider goals of sustainable and ecologically conscious pharmaceutical practices.

The following chemicals were obtained from ADWIC firm (Egypt): CuSO4·5H2O pentahydrate, NaCl, KCl, lactose monohydrate, hydroxy propyl methylcellulose (HPMC), maltose, dextrin, tartaric acid, citric acid, NH4CH3COO, avicel, H3BO3, and NaOH. An analytical grade glacial CH3COOH was acquired from AdventChemBio, located in Mumbai, India. Quinine sulphate was acquired from Alpha Chemika, a company based in Mumbai, India. Nirmatrelvir with a purity of 99.5 %, Sofosbuvir with a purity of 99.5 %, and molnupiravir with a purity of 99.5 % were supported by Sigma Pharmaceutical Industries located in El Menofia, Egypt. The Molnupiravir-EVA pharma® 200 mg capsules were obtained from a nearby drugstore with the Batch No. 2207627. The roasted seeds of the pulp were acquired from a nearby roastery.

The fluorescence measurements were conducted utilizing the JASCO FP-6300 fluorescence spectrophotometer from Japan. The Cu-CDs synthesis process involved the use of a Hettich benchtop centrifuge from Germany, a Branson 3510 ultrasonic cleaner from the USA, and a household microwave oven with a power of 1000 W. The JOEL JSM IT-00 scanning electron microscope (Tokyo, Japan) was used to perform energy-dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX). The study employed transmission electron microscopy (TEM) using a JEOL JEM-2100 HRTEM instrument operating at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV, manufactured in Japan. The XPS analysis was performed with monochromatic X-ray Al K-alpha radiation, ranging from −10 to 1350 eV, and a spot size of 400 µM. The measurements were carried out under a pressure of 10–9 mbar. The full spectrum pass energy was set at 200 eV, while the narrow spectrum pass energy was set at 50 eV. The UV absorption spectra were obtained using a Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrophotometer from Japan. FTIR spectra were obtained using a Jasco FT/IR-4000 spectrophotometer (USA). A HANNA pH211 pH-meter (USA) that has been calibrated was used to monitor pH.The X-ray diffraction “XRD” Diffractometer (GNR APD-2000 PRO, Toryno, Italy) was used to analyze the crystalline nature of Cu-CDs. It made use of a 40 kW, 40 mA spinning x-ray generator. From 5° to 80°, nanoparticle XRD data were recorded using a scanning speed of 0.3°/min and a step size of 0.02 θ.

Roasted pulp seed peels were washed, dried and grinded by a domestic miller. A weight of 1.0 g of the obtained peel powder was added to 0.1 g copper sulfate pentahydrate in 10 mL DW, well mixed then heated in domestic microwave at 1000 W for 3 min until obtaining light brown residue. After the product has been cooled to the ambient temperature, 10 mL of distilled water (DW) were introduced to the remaining substance. The mixture was then subjected to ultrasonication for a duration of 20 min to ensure homogenous mixing, followed by centrifugation at a speed of 6000 revolutions per minute (rpm) for 20 min. The liquid mixture was passed through a syringe filter with a pore size of 0.22 µm to separate the decantate. The prepared Cu-CDs were stored in the fridge at 4 °C until use. The characterization was performed using either liquid Cu-CDs for QY, TEM and HRTEM or solid form after lyophilization for XPS and EDX.

The QY was assessed relative to the quinine sulfate using the equation below:
QY
x
=
QY
st
I x
/
I
st
A
st
/
A x
η
x
2
/
η
st
2
The symbols “st” and “x” represent quinine sulphate (a fluorophore used as a reference, produced in a solution of 0.1 M H2SO4 and Cu-CDs, respectively. The refractive index of the solvent is denoted by η and has a value of 1.33 for both cases. The absorbance at 350 nm is represented by A, while the integrated fluorescence resulting from excitation at 350 nm is denoted by I [13,29]. The absorbance was kept below 0.1 in order to minimize the absorption impact, and the quantum yield (QYst) was 0.54.

In order to create a stock standard solution of molnupiravir with a concentration of 0.9 mM, 0.029 g of molnupiravir was dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water (DW). Then, 1 mL of this solution was diluted with 10 mL of DW. Different amounts of molnupiravir stock solution were combined with 100 µL of Cu-CDs solution with a concentration of 18 mg/mL. Then, 1 mL of borate buffer with a concentration of 0.05 M and a pH of 10 was added. The resulting mixture was diluted to a total volume of 3 mL using distilled water. This solution was thoroughly mixed to ensure that it covered the desired concentration range of 9–120 µM. The fluorescence of the solution was measured at a wavelength of 416 nm, with an excitation wavelength of 320 nm. The identical procedure was employed to evaluate the proposed technique's selectivity for molnupiravir in comparison to potential components that may cause interference. The emission and excitation monochromators were both set to a bandwidth of 5 nm, and a scanning speed of 1000 nm/min was employed for all fluorescence recordings.

The contents of ten capsules were mixed and a quantity equivalent to 200 mg molnupiravir was weighed, transferred to 100 mL volumetric flask, 50 mL of DW were added, sonicated for 20 min, and completed to volume with DW. The solution was filtered through 0.45 mm filter and 1500 mL of the filtrate were diluted to 10 mL with the same solvent. A volume of 100 µL of resulting solution was measured following procedure for molnupiravir determination. The procedures of molnupiravir assay in dosage forms are illustrated in Fig. 1 .

For detection of molnupiravir, tap and river water samples were collected and filtered through 0.45 mm membrane filter. A volume of 1 mL from stock solution (9 mM) was diluted to 10 mL with the water sample and 100 µL from each sample was analyzed according to the general procedure for molnupiravir determination.

A new microwave-assisted method that is both bio-compatible and straightforward for the production of CDs from roasted pulp peel has been presented. This technique offers a more efficient and less hazardous option compared to traditional approaches. The CDs produced exhibit notable optical properties. The different factors that can affect the preparation of Cu-CDs and its response to molnupiravir are discussed in the following sections, including the effect of dopant, diluent pH, CDs concentration, reaction time and mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Cu-CDs, characterization, and optical properties
ce:section.ce:para: Preparation of fluorescent CDs form biomass waste for drug analysis is a recent and innovative cost-effective approach. However, the selectivity, sensitivity and stability of these biomass-based CDs are often problematic. To overcome this problem, doping with copper was tried and the performance of the obtained CDs was compared. As shown in Fig. S1, higher emission intensities were observed in Cu-doped CDs compared with undoped CDs. In addition, no significant effect was observed in the emission spectrum of CDs when molnupiravir was added to the water soluble undoped CDs (Fig. S2). On the other hand, Cu-doped CDs showed significant quenching when mixed with molnupiravir which indicates that doping with copper affected the surface properties and enhanced the interactions potential of CDs.

The different experimental parameters that could affect the performance of Cu-CDs were studied. The effect of microwave time was investigated by testing the time from 1 to 5 min. Trying less than 3 min was not sufficient to prepare the Cu-doped CDs, while longer times in the microwave did not significantly improve the emission intensity of Cu-CDs. Accordingly, the soluble Cu-CDs were prepared using a domestic microwave in only 3 min using roasted pulp seed peels and copper as starting materials.

Fig. S3 presents the photographs of the Cu-CDs solution under both natural and UV light conditions. Upon exposure to UV light, the Cu-CDs emitted a blue fluorescence and retained this fluorescence for a duration of three weeks. The CDs that were created had a restricted size range of 3–6 nm and were seen to be uniformly distributed quasispherical particles without any noticeable clumping, as shown in the TEM images (Fig. 2 A). The d-spacing measured from the HRTEM image is about 0.26 nm, which agrees with the (100) plane lattice of graphite carbon (Fig. 2B). Zeta potential analysis in Fig. S4 showed that the Cu-CDs have a relatively stable and negative surface charge, centered around −37.59 mV. The narrow halfwidth indicates a sharp and well-defined peak, suggesting uniformity in the surface charge of the quantum dots. These graphs from the electron microscope indicated the stability and homogeneity of the nanoparticles produced through the green microwave-assisted synthesis method.

The elemental analysis of Cu-CDs was performed using EDX, as shown in Fig. 2C. The EDX spectra indicated the presence of carbon (53.22 %), oxygen (29.16 %), nitrogen (15.48 %), sulfur (1.42 %), and copper (0.72 %), validating the successful incorporation of copper into the carbon dots. This elemental distribution suggests effective doping, which is crucial for enhancing the fluorescence properties of the CDs.

Fig. 2D illustrates the FTIR spectra of the CDs both before (a) and after copper doping (b). The spectra reveal characteristic peaks corresponding to various functional groups: N–H/O–H (3418 cm−1), S–H (2620 cm−1), C=O (1636 cm−1), C–N (1388 cm−1), C–O (1184, 1100 cm−1), Cu–N (983 cm−1), and Cu–O/Cu–S (631 cm−1). These findings confirm the presence of functional groups that contribute to the CDs' stability and potential for interaction with molnupiravir. XPS analysis was performed to learn more about the chemical composition of the Cu-CDs (Fig. S5). Peaks of C1s (288.1 eV), N1s (403.1 eV), O1s (535.3 eV), S2p (156.9 eV), and Cu2p (937.6 eV) were observed in the Cu-CDs spectra. The binding energies of 291.2 eV, 286.8 eV, and 288.5 eV were found in the C1s spectra, which corresponded to graphitic pi–pi*, sp3 carbon (C–O/C–N/C–S), and C=O bonds, respectively. The peak at 288.5 is upshifted due to attachment to metal ion suggesting C–O–Cu. Three distinct peaks that corresponded to C=O, C–OH, and Cu–O bonds were seen in the O1s spectra at 534.4, 532.2, and 535.7 eV, respectively. The presence of nitroso N and Cu–N/imine bonds was indicated by two strong peaks in the N1s spectra, located at 403.4 eV and 401.5 eV. Two noticeable peaks in the S2p spectra, located at 169.9 eV and 170.9 eV, suggested the presence of thiolate species (C–S/Cu–S) and C–SO4 bonds. The Cu2p spectrum exhibited seven peaks ranging from 930 eV to 960 eV, suggesting the existence of Cu atoms in the two oxidation states, including +1 and +2 with binding energies slightly shifted suggesting strong Cu interaction with electronegative elements (O, S, N) present in the Cu-CDs. The XRD data showed high crystallinity with a peak at 32°, which corresponds to the (1 0 0) crystal plane of graphite and peaks at 45°, 56° and 75° that associated with Cu–O on the surface of the Cu-CDs (Fig. S6) [30–32]. These findings confirm the formation of Cu-CDs and the incorporation of copper on the surface.

Studying the overlap of the excitation and emission spectra of Cu-CDs is essential to exclude any possibility of self-quenching and to provide information about Stokes shift. Large Stoke’s shifts are preferable to avoid emission reabsorption by the nanoprobe itself. Fig. S7 overlays the UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence excitation/emission spectra of the Cu-CDs. The UV–Vis spectrum shows absorption peaks at 242 nm and 284 nm (π–π* transitions) in addition to 325 nm (n–π* transitions) [33,34]. The two peaks at 242 and 325 nm are hardly distinguishable in the undoped CDs (Fig. S8). The fluorescence spectrum indicates a peak emission at 430 nm when excited at 320 nm, demonstrating the excitation-dependent emission properties of the Cu-CDs, with a quantum yield of 2.7 %. Fig. S9, depicts the effect of varying excitation wavelengths on the emission intensity and wavelength of Cu-CDs. Two peaks are observed at low excitation wavelengths which could be attributed to the intrinsic π-π* transitions of the graphitic core and surface defect states introduced by Cu-doping, respectively. This excitation-dependent behavior of the CDs, highlighting their versatile optical properties, which are essential for their application as fluorescence probes.

Optimization of experimental conditions is vital to guarantee the maximum emission. Fig. S10 examines the influence of Cu-CDs concentration on the fluorescence intensity ratio. The data shows that a concentration of 0.6 mg/mL results in the highest fluorescence intensity ratio, which was subsequently used for further experiments. This optimization ensures the maximum sensitivity of the probe for detecting molnupiravir. The impact of pH was also investigated across pH values ranging from 3.0 to 11.0. The study examined how altering the pH of the diluent affects the reduction in fluorescence of Cu-CDs at a concentration of 0.6 mg/mL when exposed to molnupiravir (30 µM). The findings indicated that a pH of 10 yielded the greatest fluorescence intensity ratio of Cu-CDs. As a result, pH 10 was used in further studies, as shown in Fig. 3 . The effect of incubation time on fluorescence quenching was studied by adding molnupiravir (30 µM) to the Cu-CDs (0.6 mg/mL) (Fig. S11). The fluorescence intensity was monitored every min for a duration of 20 min. It was observed that the interaction between molnupiravir and Cu-CDs was rapid and occurred within a min. The probe's signal stayed constant for a duration of 30 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studying the sensing mechanism of molnupiravir
ce:section.ce:para:
Molnupiravir greatly reduced the fluorescence emitted by the Cu-CDs. The quenching mechanism was elucidated by employing the Stern-Volmer formula:
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
SV
Q
= 1 +
K q
τ

F represents the fluorescence intensity of the Cu-CDs in the presence of molnupiravir, and F0 represents the fluorescence intensity in the absence of molnupiravir. [Q] refers to the concentration of molnupiravir, KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant [35], Kq represents the quenching rate constant, and τ is the average lifetime of the fluorophore (10−8 s). The fluorescence quenching efficiencies (F0/F) were examined at three different temperatures (Fig. S12). The KSV exhibited a reduction as the temperature increased, indicating a static quenching mechanism. The values of KSV were 9.15 × 103, 7.80 × 103, and 4.11 × 103 L/mol at temperatures of 298, 323, and 343 K, respectively. The calculated values for Kq were 9.15 × 1011, 7.80 × 1011, and 4.11 × 1011 L.mol−1.s−1, respectively. The observation that Kq markedly exceeded the maximum diffusion-controlled quenching rate constant (2.0 × 1010 L.mol−1.s−1) further supports a static quenching mechanism, as dynamic quenching is typically limited by diffusion rates.

To confirm complex formation, UV–Vis spectral analysis was conducted (Fig. S13). A new absorption peak at 259 nm was observed after the addition of molnupiravir, indicating the formation of a ground-state complex between Cu-CDs and molnupiravir. This conclusion is reinforced by the differences between the absorption spectra of the Cu-CD-molnupiravir mixture and molnupiravir alone, with the mixture showing a distinct spectral shift, confirming molecular interactions. Thus, the fluorescence quenching of Cu-CDs by molnupiravir occurs through a static quenching mechanism, driven by complex formation rather than collisional interactions, as confirmed by both Stern-Volmer analysis and UV–Vis spectroscopy.

The developed probe was used to analyze the drug in Molnupiravir-EVA pharma® 200 mg capsules. Sample solutions were created and assessed by following the processes outlined in the “Pharmaceutical formulation analysis” section. The findings were shown in Table 2 and statistical comparisons were conducted with the results of the comparative approach [8]. The statistical analysis highlighted the probe's constant determination of MOL. This cross-validation of the method proves the method’s selectivity for the determination of MOL in pharmaceutical capsules with no interference from pharmaceutical excipients and additives.

The developed sensor was further implied in the determination of molnupiravir in two real water samples. Samples collected from tap and river water sources were subjected to testing for the presence of molnupiravir. The analysis of these samples was conducted in accordance with the methodology outlined in section 2.6, titled “Procedure for Real Sample Analysis.” The results, including the percentage recoveries derived from the corresponding regression equation and the percentage relative standard deviation (RSD), are presented in Table S3. The data indicate that the percentage recoveries fall within the acceptable limits, while the RSD values reflect the precision of the analytical method employed. These results indicate that the method is sufficiently selective for the determination of MOL in dosage forms and in real environmental water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green assessment of the proposed method
ce:section.ce:para: The environmental sustainability of the Cu-CDs based method was evaluated using AGREEprep for the sample preparation procedure and AGREE for the analytical procedure while its practicality was assessed via the BAGI index. These assessments aimed to determine the method's environmental compatibility and effectiveness. The pictograms in Fig. 5 illustrate the method's greenness. AGREEprep indicates that the method is environmentally friendly except for the first parameter (in situ sample preparation), as the ample preparation is performed in the laboratory after sample collection and transportation. Additionally, AGREEprep highlights that the method involves multiple manual steps before the sample is ready for analysis. The BAGI pictogram demonstrates the method's practicality. A noted limitation is that the method provides single-component analysis according to the 2nd parameter of BAGI. The method’s automation degree of automation is a point that can be improved in the future to facilitate the workflow. However, this is because the method is selective in the first place to molnupiravir determination. Despite these issues, the overall greenness and practicality assessments indicate that the method is environmentally friendly and reliable for its intended purpose.

Various analytical techniques have been reported for the detection and quantification of molnupiravir and its metabolites, including LC–MS/MS, spectrophotometry, micellar HPLC, HPTLC, and Spectrofluorometry. To compare the performance of the developed method, each reported method was evaluated based on linearity, limit of detection (LOD), analytical eco-scale, and MoGAPI [38]. The LC–MS/MS exhibits exceptional sensitivity and specificity, making it well-suited for analyzing complicated biological samples. However, this approach is difficult and requires specialized equipment that is not often found in most analytical laboratories. Spectrophotometry is a straightforward and economical method, although it is hindered by its restricted sensitivity and susceptibility to interference. Micellar HPLC compromises the longevity and performance of the column. The restricted sensitivity and specificity of HPTLC hinder its practicality. Spectrofluorometry stands out as the most beneficial technique, demonstrating exceptional sensitivity, outstanding selectivity, and good eco-scale scores. The advantages of the Cu-CDs technology are a direct result of its little environmental impact. The approach proposed also encompasses wide linear ranges and achieves low limits of detection (LODs), rendering it appropriate for precise and environmentally friendly analysis of molnupiravir in pharmaceutical matrices. The exceptional selectivity of spectrofluorometry is reinforced by the specific interaction between Cu-CDs and molnupiravir, even in the presence of co-formulated substances. Fig. S15 summarizes the major differences between the developed method and the other reported methods for molnupiravir determination.

This study introduces a novel, rapid, and eco-friendly spectrofluorometric method for the determination of molnupiravir in pharmaceutical capsules, utilizing copper-doped carbon dots (Cu-CDs) synthesized from biowaste via a simple microwave-assisted process. The Cu-CDs exhibit exceptional fluorescence properties, enabling highly sensitive and selective detection of molnupiravir through a static quenching mechanism. The method achieved a broad linearity range, high recovery rates, and low detection limits, validating its precision, accuracy, and robustness. By valorizing biomass waste and employing green synthesis techniques, this sustainable approach adheres to green chemistry principles and minimizes environmental impact compared to conventional methods that rely on hazardous reagents and resource-intensive instrumentation. Additionally, the Cu-CDs were shown to be stable, uniform, and effective in complex matrices, demonstrating their versatility and reliability as fluorescence probes. The rapid microwave-assisted synthesis significantly reduced reaction times to mere minutes, compared with the hours required by hydrothermal or other traditional approaches. This method not only provides a cost-effective and accessible solution for pharmaceutical quality control but also emphasizes the potential for integrating environmentally conscious practices in analytical chemistry. By advancing the field of antiviral drug analysis, this work highlights the capability of nanotechnology and green methodologies to meet urgent analytical needs while promoting sustainability. Future research could further explore the broader applications of Cu-CDs in the analysis of other pharmaceutical compounds and environmental monitoring. This approach reinforces the value of Cu-CDs as a versatile and eco-friendly analytical tool.

Eman A. Elshenawy: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Fotouh R. Mansour: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.113003.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Public health actions to control new SARS-CoV-2 variants
Cell

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N.D. Grubaugh, E.B. Hodcroft, J.R. Fauver, A.L. Phelan, M. Cevik, Public health actions to control new SARS-CoV-2 variants, Cell 184 (2021) 1127–1132. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cell.2021.01.044.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The potential roles of Ficus carica extract in the management of COVID-19 viral infections: a computer-aided drug design study
